7.1: The men of Kiriath-jearim came and brought up the ark of the LORD and took it into the house of Abinadab on the hill, consecrating his son Eleazar to guard the ark of the LORD.
7.2: From the day the ark remained in Kiriath-jearim a long time passed—twenty years—and all the house of Israel mourned and yearned after the LORD.
7.3: Samuel said to all the house of Israel, 'If you are returning to the LORD with all your hearts, then remove the foreign gods and the Ashtaroth from among you, direct your hearts to the LORD, and serve him alone—he will rescue you from the hand of the Philistines.'
7.4: So the Israelites put away the Baals and the Ashtaroth and served the LORD alone.
7.5: Samuel said, 'Assemble all Israel at Mizpah, and I will intercede for you before the LORD.'
7.6: They assembled at Mizpah, drew water and poured it out before the LORD, fasted that day, and declared there, 'We have sinned against the LORD.' And Samuel judged the Israelites at Mizpah.
7.7: When the Philistine lords heard that the Israelites had assembled at Mizpah, they marched up against Israel. The Israelites, hearing this, were afraid of the Philistines.
7.8: The Israelites said to Samuel, 'Do not stop crying out to the LORD our God for us, so that he may save us from the hand of the Philistines.'
7.9: Samuel took a nursing lamb and offered it as a whole burnt offering to the LORD, and cried out to the LORD on Israel's behalf—and the LORD answered him.
7.10: As Samuel was offering the burnt offering, the Philistines advanced to attack Israel. But the LORD thundered with a great thunder against the Philistines that day, throwing them into panic, and they were routed before Israel.
7.11: The men of Israel marched out from Mizpah, pursued the Philistines, and struck them down all the way to below Beth-car.
7.12: Samuel took a stone and set it up between Mizpah and Shen, naming it Ebenezer, saying, 'To this point the LORD has helped us.'
7.13: The Philistines were brought low and did not again enter Israelite territory. The hand of the LORD was against the Philistines throughout Samuel's lifetime.
7.14: The towns the Philistines had seized from Israel were restored, from Ekron to Gath, and Israel recovered their surrounding territories from Philistine control. There was also peace between Israel and the Amorites.
7.15: Samuel judged Israel throughout his lifetime.
7.16: Each year he made a circuit to Bethel, Gilgal, and Mizpah, judging Israel at each of those sites.
7.17: His home base was Ramah, where he lived, judged Israel, and built an altar to the LORD.

8.1: When Samuel grew old, he appointed his sons as judges over Israel.
8.2: His firstborn was named Joel and his second son Abijah; they served as judges at Beersheba.
8.3: But his sons did not follow his example—they turned to greed, accepted bribes, and twisted justice.
8.4: All the elders of Israel assembled and came to Samuel at Ramah,
8.5: They said to him, 'You are old, and your sons do not follow your ways. Appoint a king for us to rule us, as all the other nations have.'
8.6: The request displeased Samuel when they said, 'Give us a king to judge us.' Samuel prayed to the LORD.
8.7: The LORD said to Samuel, 'Listen to the voice of the people in everything they say to you; for they have not rejected you, but they have rejected me from reigning over them.'
8.8: This is consistent with everything they have done since the day I brought them up out of Egypt to this day—forsaking me and serving other gods. Now they are doing the same to you.
8.9: So comply with their request; but first solemnly warn them and describe to them the practice of the king who will reign over them.
8.10: Samuel reported all the LORD's words to the people who had asked him for a king.
8.11: He said, 'This is what the king who reigns over you will do: he will take your sons and assign them to his chariot corps and cavalry, with some running before his chariots.'
8.12: He will appoint commanders over thousands and fifties, and will put your sons to work plowing his fields, harvesting his crops, and making his weapons and chariot equipment.
8.13: He will take your daughters to serve as perfumers, cooks, and bakers.
8.14: He will seize your best fields, vineyards, and olive groves and hand them over to his officials.
8.15: He will tithe your grain and your vineyards, distributing the proceeds among his officers and staff.
8.16: He will take your male and female servants, your finest young men, and your donkeys, and put them all to work for himself.
8.17: He will take a tenth of your flocks, and you will become his subjects.
8.18: In that day you will cry out because of the king you have chosen for yourselves, but the LORD will not answer you.
8.19: But the people refused to listen to Samuel and said, 'No! We will have a king over us—'
8.20: '—so that we may be like all the other nations, with our king to rule us, lead us out, and fight our battles.'
8.21: Samuel listened to all the people's words and then brought them before the LORD.
8.22: The LORD told Samuel, 'Listen to their voice and give them a king.' Then Samuel said to the men of Israel, 'Each of you, return to your town.'

9.1: There was a Benjaminite named Kish son of Abiel son of Zeror son of Bechorath son of Aphiah—a Benjaminite and a man of great standing.
9.2: He had a son named Saul, a fine-looking young man—no one among the Israelites was more handsome; from his shoulders up he stood taller than all the people.
9.3: The donkeys of Kish, Saul's father, had gone astray. Kish told his son Saul, 'Take one of the servants and go look for the donkeys.'
9.4: He traveled through the hill country of Ephraim and through the region of Shalisha, but they did not find the donkeys. They went through Shalim—not there either. They crossed the land of Benjamin—still nothing.
9.5: When they reached the land of Zuph, Saul said to his servant, 'Let's go back, or my father will stop worrying about the donkeys and start worrying about us.'
9.6: The servant replied, 'Look, in this town there is a man of God—a man of distinction whose every word proves true. Let's go there; perhaps he can tell us which way we should go.'
9.7: Saul replied to his servant, 'But if we go, what can we bring the man? Our food is gone, and we have no gift to offer the man of God. What do we have?'
9.8: The servant replied, 'I happen to have a quarter shekel of silver with me. I'll give that to the man of God and he can tell us which way to go.'
9.9: (In former times in Israel, anyone seeking to inquire of God would say, 'Come, let us go to the seer,' since the one now called a prophet was then called a seer.)
9.10: Saul replied, 'Good idea—let's go.' So they headed for the town where the man of God lived.
9.11: As they climbed the approach to the town, they met some young women going out to draw water and asked them, 'Is the seer here?'
9.12: They replied, 'He is—in fact, he's right ahead of you. Hurry, because he came to town today for a sacrifice the people are holding at the high place.'
9.13: When you enter the town you'll find him right away, before he goes up to the high place to eat. The people won't eat until he arrives, because he pronounces the blessing over the sacrifice; only then do the guests eat. Go now—you'll find him at this very hour.
9.14: They went into the town, and as they entered, Samuel was coming out toward them on his way up to the high place.
9.15: The LORD had disclosed to Samuel the day before Saul arrived, saying,
9.16: 'Tomorrow about this time I will send you a man from Benjamin—anoint him as ruler over my people Israel, to save them from the hand of the Philistines. I have seen the suffering of my people and their cry has come up to me.'
9.17: When Samuel saw Saul, the LORD said to him, 'Here is the man I told you about—he will govern my people.'
9.18: Saul approached Samuel at the gate and said, 'Excuse me—could you tell me where the seer's house is?'
9.19: Samuel replied, 'I am the seer. Come up ahead of me to the high place—you will dine with me today, and tomorrow I will send you on your way and tell you everything that is on your mind.'
9.20: As for the donkeys you lost three days ago—don't worry about them; they have been found. And as for whom all Israel desires—is it not you, and your whole family?
9.21: Saul answered, 'But I'm a Benjaminite—from the smallest tribe in Israel! My family is the least of all Benjamin's clans. Why do you speak to me like this?'
9.22: Samuel brought Saul and his servant into the banquet hall and seated them at the head of the table among the roughly thirty invited guests.
9.23: Samuel instructed the cook, 'Bring out the portion I gave you—the one I told you to set aside.'
9.24: The cook lifted up the thigh portion and what was on it and placed it before Saul. Samuel said, 'Look—this has been reserved for you. Eat it, for it was set aside for you until this moment, from the time I invited the guests.' So Saul ate with Samuel that day.
9.25: When they came down from the high place to the town, Samuel talked with Saul on the roof of the house.
9.26: They rose early, and at dawn Samuel called up to Saul on the roof, 'Up—I'll send you on your way.' Saul got up and the two of them went out together.
9.27: As they reached the edge of town, Samuel told Saul, 'Send the servant on ahead of us'—and he did—'but you stay here a moment, so I can make the word of God known to you.'

10.1: Samuel took a flask of oil, poured it over his head, and kissed him, saying, 'The LORD has anointed you ruler over his inheritance!'
10.2: When you leave me today, you will meet two men near Rachel's tomb in Benjaminite territory at Zelzah. They will tell you, 'The donkeys you were looking for have been found, but now your father has stopped thinking about the donkeys and is worried about you, asking, "What's become of my son?"'
10.3: From there you will go on to the oak of Tabor, where you will meet three men on their way up to God at Bethel—one carrying three young goats, another three loaves of bread, and another a skin of wine.
10.4: They will greet you and offer you two of their loaves of bread, which you must take from them.
10.5: After that you will come to Gibeah-elohim, where the Philistine garrison is. As you enter the town, you will meet a group of prophets coming down from the high place playing harp, tambourine, flute, and lyre—all prophesying.
10.6: The Spirit of the LORD will come powerfully upon you, and you will prophesy with them and be changed into a different person.
10.7: Once these signs have occurred, act as the situation demands—for God is with you.
10.8: Then go down to Gilgal ahead of me. I will come down to you there to offer burnt offerings and peace offerings. Wait seven days until I arrive—I will tell you what to do next.
10.9: As Saul turned to leave Samuel, God gave him a new heart, and all the signs came to pass that day.
10.10: When they arrived at the hill, a group of prophets came to meet him. The Spirit of God came powerfully upon him, and he prophesied among them.
10.11: When everyone who had known him before saw that he was prophesying with the prophets, people said to one another, 'What has come over the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets?'
10.12: Someone from that place answered, 'And who is their father?' That is how the saying originated: 'Is Saul also among the prophets?'
10.13: When he had finished prophesying, he went to the high place.
10.14: Saul's uncle asked him and his servant, 'Where did you go?' He replied, 'Looking for the donkeys. When we couldn't find them, we went to Samuel.'
10.15: His uncle pressed him, 'Tell me what Samuel said to you.'
10.16: Saul replied, 'He told us clearly that the donkeys had been found.' But the matter of the kingship that Samuel had spoken of, Saul said nothing.
10.17: Samuel summoned the people before the LORD at Mizpah.
10.18: He said to the Israelites, 'This is what the LORD, the God of Israel, says: I brought Israel up out of Egypt and rescued you from the power of Egypt and all the kingdoms that were oppressing you.'
10.19: But today you have rejected your God—the one who has saved you from every calamity and distress—and have said to him, 'No, put a king over us.' Now, then, present yourselves before the LORD by tribes and by thousands.
10.20: Samuel brought all the tribes of Israel forward, and the tribe of Benjamin was chosen by lot.
10.21: He brought Benjamin forward by clans, and the clan of Matri was chosen; then Saul son of Kish was chosen. But when they looked for him, he was nowhere to be found.
10.22: They consulted the LORD further, asking, 'Has the man arrived yet?' The LORD replied, 'He has hidden himself among the luggage.'
10.23: They ran and brought him out; when he stood among the people, he was a head taller than everyone else.
10.24: Samuel said to all the people, 'Do you see who the LORD has chosen? There is no one like him in all the people.' The people all shouted, 'Long live the king!'
10.25: Samuel then explained to the people the rights and duties of the kingship, wrote them in a document, and deposited it before the LORD. He dismissed all the people, each to their own home.
10.26: Saul went to his home at Gibeah, accompanied by men whose hearts God had moved.
10.27: But certain worthless men said, 'How is this man going to save us?' They despised him and brought him no gift. But Saul said nothing.

11.1: Nahash the Ammonite came up and besieged Jabesh-gilead. The men of Jabesh said to him, 'Make a treaty with us and we will serve you.'
11.2: Nahash replied, 'I will make terms on one condition: I will gouge out the right eye of every one of you, and thereby shame all Israel.'
11.3: The elders of Jabesh said, 'Grant us a seven-day delay to send messengers throughout Israel; if no one comes to rescue us, we will surrender to you.'
11.4: The messengers came to Gibeah of Saul and reported the news to the people. The entire town raised their voices and wept.
11.5: Just then Saul was coming in from the field behind his oxen. 'What is wrong with the people?' he asked. 'Why are they weeping?' They told him what the men of Jabesh faced.
11.6: The Spirit of God came powerfully upon Saul when he heard those words, and he burned with fury.
11.7: He took a yoke of oxen, cut them in pieces, and sent the pieces throughout Israel by messenger, declaring, 'This is what will be done to the oxen of any man who does not march out with Saul and Samuel.' The terror of the LORD fell on the people and they turned out as one.
11.8: He mustered them at Bezek: three hundred thousand Israelites and thirty thousand from Judah.
11.9: They told the messengers, 'Say this to the men of Jabesh-gilead: By tomorrow at noon you will be rescued.' When the messengers returned and told them, the men of Jabesh were overjoyed.
11.10: So the men of Jabesh told Nahash, 'Tomorrow we will come out to you, and you may do with us whatever you see fit.'
11.11: The next morning Saul divided the force into three columns. They attacked the Ammonite camp in the last watch of the night and struck them down until the heat of the day. The survivors were so scattered that no two were left together.
11.12: The people said to Samuel, 'Who said, "Shall Saul reign over us?" Hand those men over—we'll put them to death.'
11.13: But Saul said, 'No one shall be put to death today, for today the LORD has given Israel victory.'
11.14: Samuel said to the people, 'Let us go to Gilgal and reaffirm the kingship there.'
11.15: All the people went to Gilgal, where they crowned Saul king before the LORD. They offered peace offerings before the LORD, and Saul and all the Israelites rejoiced greatly.

12.1: Samuel addressed all Israel: 'I have listened to you in everything you asked of me and have set a king over you.'
12.2: See, the king now leads you—and I am old and gray. My sons are here with you. I have served before you from my youth until this day.
12.3: Here I stand. Testify against me before the LORD and before his anointed. Have I taken anyone's ox or donkey? Have I defrauded or oppressed anyone? Have I taken a bribe to turn a blind eye? If so, I will make it right.
12.4: They replied, 'You have not defrauded us, oppressed us, or taken anything from anyone.'
12.5: He said to them, 'The LORD and his anointed are witnesses today that you have found nothing in my hands.' They answered, 'He is witness.'
12.6: Samuel said to the people, 'It is the LORD who appointed Moses and Aaron and brought your ancestors up out of Egypt.'
12.7: Now take your stand, so I may lay before you before the LORD all the LORD's righteous deeds on your behalf and your ancestors' behalf.
12.8: When Jacob went to Egypt and your ancestors cried out to the LORD, the LORD sent Moses and Aaron, who brought them out of Egypt and settled them in this land.
12.9: But when they forgot the LORD their God, he handed them over to Sisera commander of Hazor's army, to the Philistines, and to the king of Moab, and these nations fought against them.
12.10: They cried out to the LORD and confessed, 'We have sinned—we forsook the LORD and served the Baals and the Ashtaroth. Deliver us now from our enemies and we will serve you.'
12.11: The LORD sent Jerubbaal, Bedan, Jephthah, and Samuel, and rescued you from your enemies all around—and you lived in safety.
12.12: But when you saw Nahash king of Ammon advancing against you, you said to me, 'No—we want a king to rule us!'—even though the LORD your God was your king.
12.13: Now here is the king you chose and asked for. The LORD has set a king over you.
12.14: If you fear the LORD and serve him and obey his voice and do not rebel against his command, then both you and the king who rules you will follow the LORD your God—
12.15: —but if you will not obey the LORD and instead rebel against his command, the hand of the LORD will be against you, as it was against your ancestors.
12.16: Now then, stand still and see the great thing the LORD is about to do before your eyes.
12.17: Isn't this the wheat harvest? I will call on the LORD, and he will send thunder and rain—so that you will understand how great your wickedness was before the LORD in asking for a king.
12.18: Samuel called on the LORD, and the LORD sent thunder and rain that day. All the people stood in deep awe of the LORD and of Samuel.
12.19: All the people said to Samuel, 'Pray to the LORD your God for us—your servants—so we won't die! We have added to all our sins this evil of asking for a king.'
12.20: Samuel replied, 'Do not be afraid. You have committed all this wrong, but do not turn away from the LORD—serve him with your whole heart.'
12.21: Do not turn away to pursue empty idols that cannot help or save—they are worthless.
12.22: For the LORD will not abandon his people, because of his great name—it pleased him to make you his own.
12.23: As for me, far be it from me to sin against the LORD by stopping my prayers for you. I will teach you the good and right way.
12.24: Only fear the LORD and serve him faithfully with all your heart—consider the great things he has done for you.
12.25: But if you persist in doing evil, both you and your king will be swept away.

1.1: There was a certain man from Ramathaim-zophim in the hill country of Ephraim, whose name was Elkanah son of Jeroham, son of Elihu, son of Tohu, son of Zuph, an Ephraimite.
1.2: He had two wives. The name of one was Hannah and the name of the other Peninnah. Peninnah had children, but Hannah had no children.
1.3: This man went up from his city every year to worship and sacrifice to the LORD of hosts at Shiloh, where Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, served as priests of the LORD.
1.4: On the day Elkanah offered sacrifice, he would give portions to his wife Peninnah and to all her sons and daughters.
1.5: But to Hannah he gave a double portion, because he loved her, even though the LORD had closed her womb.
1.6: Her rival would provoke her bitterly to irritate her, because the LORD had closed her womb.
1.7: This went on year after year. Whenever Hannah went up to the house of the LORD, Peninnah would provoke her, so that she wept and would not eat.
1.8: Elkanah her husband said to her, "Hannah, why are you weeping? Why won't you eat? Why is your heart grieved? Am I not more to you than ten sons?"
1.9: After they had eaten and drunk in Shiloh, Hannah rose. Now Eli the priest was sitting on his seat by the doorpost of the temple of the LORD.
1.10: She was deeply distressed and prayed to the LORD, weeping bitterly.
1.11: And she made a vow, saying, "O LORD of hosts, if you will look on the misery of your servant and remember me and not forget me, but will give me a son, then I will give him to the LORD all the days of his life, and no razor shall touch his head."
1.12: As she continued praying before the LORD, Eli watched her mouth.
1.13: Hannah was speaking in her heart; only her lips moved, and her voice was not heard. Therefore Eli took her to be a drunken woman.
1.14: And Eli said to her, "How long are you going to be drunk? Put your wine away."
1.15: But Hannah answered, "No, my lord, I am a woman deeply troubled in spirit. I have drunk neither wine nor strong drink, but I have been pouring out my soul before the LORD.
1.16: Do not take your servant for a worthless woman, for I have been speaking from my great anxiety and grief all this time."
1.17: Then Eli answered, "Go in peace, and may the God of Israel grant your petition that you have asked of him."
1.18: And she said, "Let your servant find favor in your eyes." So the woman went her way, and she ate and drank, and her face was no longer downcast.
1.19: They rose early in the morning and worshiped before the LORD, then returned to their home at Ramah. Elkanah lay with Hannah his wife, and the LORD remembered her.
1.20: In the course of time Hannah conceived and bore a son, and she called his name Samuel, for she said, "I have asked for him from the LORD."
1.21: The man Elkanah and all his household went up to offer the yearly sacrifice to the LORD and to fulfill his vow.
1.22: But Hannah did not go up, for she said to her husband, "As soon as the child is weaned, I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD and remain there permanently."
1.23: Elkanah her husband said to her, "Do what seems best to you; wait until you have weaned him. Only, may the LORD establish his word." So the woman remained and nursed her son until she weaned him.
1.24: And when she had weaned him, she took him up with her, along with a three-year-old bull, an ephah of flour, and a skin of wine, and she brought him to the house of the LORD at Shiloh. The child was still young.
1.25: They slaughtered the bull and brought the child to Eli.
1.26: And she said, "Oh, my lord! As you live, my lord, I am the woman who was standing here in your presence, praying to the LORD.
1.27: For this child I prayed, and the LORD has granted me my petition that I asked of him.
1.28: Therefore I have given him back to the LORD. As long as he lives, he belongs to the LORD." And he worshiped the LORD there.

2.1: And Hannah prayed and said: "My heart rejoices in the LORD; my horn is lifted high in the LORD. My mouth speaks boldly against my enemies, for I rejoice in your deliverance.
2.2: There is no one holy like the LORD; there is no one besides you; there is no rock like our God.
2.3: Do not speak with such great pride; let no arrogance come from your mouth, for the LORD is a God of knowledge, and by him deeds are weighed.
2.4: The bows of the mighty are shattered, but those who stumbled are girded with strength.
2.5: Those who were full have hired themselves out for bread, but those who were hungry are hungry no more. The barren woman has borne seven, but she who has many children languishes.
2.6: The LORD brings death and gives life; he brings down to Sheol and raises up.
2.7: The LORD makes poor and makes rich; he brings low, and he also lifts up.
2.8: He raises the poor from the dust and lifts the needy from the ash heap, to seat them with princes and give them a throne of honor. For the pillars of the earth are the LORD's, and on them he has set the world.
2.9: He will guard the feet of his faithful ones, but the wicked will be silenced in darkness, for not by strength does a man prevail.
2.10: The LORD's enemies shall be shattered; against them he will thunder in heaven. The LORD will judge the ends of the earth; he will give strength to his king and lift high the horn of his anointed.
2.11: Then Elkanah went home to Ramah, and the boy remained to minister to the LORD in the presence of Eli the priest.
2.12: Now the sons of Eli were worthless men; they did not know the LORD.
2.13: The priests' practice with the people was this: when anyone offered a sacrifice, the priest's servant would come while the meat was boiling, with a three-pronged fork in his hand,
2.14: and he would thrust it into the pan, kettle, cauldron, or pot. Everything the fork brought up the priest would take for himself. This is what they did to all the Israelites who came to Shiloh.
2.15: Even before the fat was burned, the priest's servant would come and say to the man who was sacrificing, "Give the priest meat to roast, for he will not accept boiled meat—only raw."
2.16: And if the man said, "Let the fat be burned first, and then take whatever you want," he would say, "No, hand it over now; if not, I will take it by force."
2.17: The sin of these young men was very great in the sight of the LORD, for they treated the LORD's offering with contempt.
2.18: Now Samuel was ministering before the LORD, a young boy clothed in a linen ephod.
2.19: And his mother would make a little robe for him and bring it up to him each year when she came with her husband to offer the yearly sacrifice.
2.20: And Eli would bless Elkanah and his wife, saying, "May the LORD give you children by this woman in return for the gift she has given to the LORD." Then they would go back to their home.
2.21: Indeed the LORD visited Hannah, and she conceived and bore three sons and two daughters. And the young Samuel grew up in the presence of the LORD.
2.22: Now Eli was very old, and he kept hearing about everything his sons were doing to all Israel, and how they were sleeping with the women who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting.
2.23: And he said to them, "Why do you do such things? For I hear of your evil deeds from all these people.
2.24: No, my sons, it is not a good report that I hear the LORD's people spreading.
2.25: "If one person sins against another, God can mediate for him, but if someone sins against the LORD, who will intercede for him?" But they would not listen to the voice of their father, for it was the will of the LORD to put them to death.
2.26: The young man Samuel continued to grow and to be in favor with the LORD and with men.
2.27: And a man of God came to Eli and said to him, "Thus says the LORD: 'Did I not reveal myself to your father's house when they were in Egypt under Pharaoh?
2.28: I chose your ancestor from all the tribes of Israel to be my priest, to go up to my altar, to burn incense, and to wear an ephod before me. I gave the house of your father all the offerings made by fire from the people of Israel.
2.29: Why then do you despise my sacrifices and my offerings that I have commanded, and honor your sons more than me by fattening yourselves on the best parts of every offering of my people Israel?'
2.30: Therefore the LORD, the God of Israel, declares: 'I had said that your house and your father's house would walk before me forever, but now the LORD declares: Far be it from me! For those who honor me I will honor, and those who despise me will be treated with contempt.
2.31: See, the days are coming when I will cut off your strength and the strength of your father's house, so that no man in your house will reach old age.
2.32: You will look with envy at all the good that will be done for Israel, but there will never be an old man in your house again.
2.33: The one of yours I do not cut off from my altar will be left to consume your eyes with grief and break your heart, and all the other descendants of your house will die by the sword.
2.34: And this will be the sign for you: both your sons, Hophni and Phinehas, shall die on the same day.
2.35: And I will raise up for myself a faithful priest who will do what is in my heart and mind. I will build him an enduring house, and he will walk before my anointed one always.
2.36: And everyone left in your house will come to beg him for a piece of silver or a loaf of bread and say, 'Please assign me to one of the priestly offices so that I may eat a piece of bread.'"

3.1: Now the young Samuel was ministering to the LORD under Eli. The word of the LORD was rare in those days; visions were not frequent.
3.2: At that time Eli, whose eyesight had grown so dim that he could no longer see, was lying down in his room.
3.3: The lamp of God had not yet gone out, and Samuel was lying down in the temple of the LORD, near where the ark of God was.
3.4: Then the LORD called, "Samuel!" and he said, "Here I am."
3.5: He ran to Eli and said, "Here I am, for you called me." But Eli said, "I did not call you; lie down again." So he went and lay down.
3.6: The LORD called again, "Samuel!" And Samuel arose and went to Eli and said, "Here I am, for you called me." But Eli said, "I did not call, my son; lie down again."
3.7: Now Samuel did not yet know the LORD, and the word of the LORD had not yet been revealed to him.
3.8: The LORD called Samuel again the third time. He arose and went to Eli and said, "Here I am, for you called me." Then Eli understood that the LORD was calling the boy.
3.9: Therefore Eli said to Samuel, "Go, lie down, and if he calls you, you shall say, 'Speak, LORD, for your servant is listening.'" So Samuel went and lay down in his place.
3.10: The LORD came and stood there, calling as before: "Samuel! Samuel!" And Samuel said, "Speak, for your servant is listening."
3.11: The LORD said to Samuel, "Behold, I am about to do something in Israel that will make the ears of everyone who hears it tingle.
3.12: On that day I will carry out against Eli everything I have spoken concerning his house, from beginning to end.
3.13: For I have told him that I am about to judge his house forever for the iniquity that he knew, because his sons were blaspheming God and he did not rebuke them.
3.14: Therefore I swear to the house of Eli that the iniquity of Eli's house shall never be atoned for by sacrifice or offering.
3.15: Samuel lay there until morning, then opened the doors of the house of the LORD. And Samuel was afraid to tell Eli the vision.
3.16: Then Eli called Samuel and said, "Samuel, my son." And he said, "Here I am."
3.17: Eli said, "What did he say to you? Do not hide it from me. May God deal with you severely if you hide from me anything he said to you."
3.18: So Samuel told him everything and hid nothing from him. And Eli said, "He is the LORD. Let him do what seems right in his eyes."
3.19: Samuel grew, and the LORD was with him and let none of his words fall to the ground.
3.20: And all Israel from Dan to Beersheba knew that Samuel had been established as a prophet of the LORD.
3.21: And the LORD continued to appear at Shiloh, for the LORD revealed himself to Samuel at Shiloh through the word of the LORD.

4.1: And the word of Samuel came to all Israel. Now Israel went out to fight against the Philistines. They encamped at Ebenezer, and the Philistines encamped at Aphek.
4.2: The Philistines drew up in line against Israel, and when the battle spread, Israel was defeated before the Philistines, who killed about four thousand men on the field.
4.3: When the troops came back to camp, the elders of Israel said, "Why has the LORD defeated us today before the Philistines? Let us bring the ark of the covenant of the LORD here from Shiloh, that it may come among us and save us from the power of our enemies."
4.4: So the people sent to Shiloh and brought from there the ark of the covenant of the LORD of hosts, who is enthroned between the cherubim. And Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the ark of the covenant of God.
4.5: When the ark of the covenant of the LORD came into the camp, all Israel raised such a great shout that the ground shook.
4.6: When the Philistines heard the noise of the shouting, they asked, "What is this great shouting in the Hebrew camp?" And when they learned that the ark of the LORD had come to the camp,
4.7: the Philistines were afraid, for they said, "A god has come into the camp!" And they said, "Woe to us! Nothing like this has ever happened before.
4.8: Woe to us! Who will deliver us from the hand of these mighty gods? These are the gods who struck the Egyptians with every kind of plague in the wilderness.
4.9: "Be strong and act like men, O Philistines, lest you become slaves to the Hebrews as they have been to you. Fight, and be men!"
4.10: So the Philistines fought, and Israel was defeated, and every man fled to his home. The slaughter was very great; thirty thousand Israelite foot soldiers fell.
4.11: The ark of God was captured, and Eli's two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, died.
4.12: A man from Benjamin ran from the battle line and reached Shiloh the same day, with his clothes torn and dust on his head.
4.13: When he arrived, Eli was sitting on his seat beside the road watching, for his heart trembled with anxiety over the ark of God. When the man came into the city and reported the news, the whole city cried out.
4.14: When Eli heard the noise of the outcry, he said, "What is the meaning of this commotion?" The man hurried to Eli and told him.
4.15: Now Eli was ninety-eight years old, and his eyes were fixed and he could not see.
4.16: And the man said to Eli, "I am the one who has come from the battle; I fled from the battle today." Eli said, "How did it go, my son?"
4.17: The messenger answered, "Israel has fled before the Philistines, and there has been a great slaughter among the people. Your two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, are dead, and the ark of God has been captured."
4.18: The moment he mentioned the ark of God, Eli fell backward off his seat by the side of the gate, and his neck broke and he died, for the man was old and heavy. He had judged Israel forty years.
4.19: Now his daughter-in-law, the wife of Phinehas, was pregnant and near her time. When she heard the news that the ark of God was captured and that her father-in-law and her husband were dead, she went into labor and crouched down, for her birth pains came upon her.
4.20: And at the moment of her death, the women attending her said to her, "Do not be afraid, for you have given birth to a son." But she did not answer or take notice.
4.21: She named the child Ichabod, saying, "The glory has departed from Israel"—because the ark of God was captured and because of her father-in-law and her husband.
4.22: She said, "The glory has departed from Israel, for the ark of God has been captured."

5.1: When the Philistines captured the ark of God, they brought it from Ebenezer to Ashdod.
5.2: Then the Philistines took the ark of God and brought it into the temple of Dagon and placed it beside Dagon.
5.3: But when the people of Ashdod rose early the next day, Dagon had fallen face downward on the ground before the ark of the LORD. So they took Dagon and returned him to his place.
5.4: But when they got up early the next morning, Dagon had again fallen face downward on the ground before the ark of the LORD, and the head of Dagon and both his hands were lying cut off on the threshold. Only the torso of Dagon was left.
5.5: This is why to this day neither the priests of Dagon nor any who enter the house of Dagon tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod.
5.6: The hand of the LORD was heavy against the people of Ashdod. He devastated them and struck them with tumors—Ashdod and its surrounding territory.
5.7: When the men of Ashdod saw what was happening, they said, "The ark of the God of Israel must not stay with us, for his hand is harsh against us and against Dagon our god."
5.8: So they sent and gathered all the lords of the Philistines and said, "What shall we do with the ark of the God of Israel?" They answered, "Let the ark of the God of Israel be moved to Gath." So they moved the ark of the God of Israel to Gath.
5.9: But after they brought it to Gath, the hand of the LORD was against that city, causing a very great panic. He struck the men of the city, both young and old, with tumors.
5.10: So they sent the ark of God to Ekron. But when the ark of God arrived in Ekron, the Ekronites cried out, "They have brought the ark of the God of Israel to us to kill us and our people!"
5.11: So they sent and gathered all the lords of the Philistines and said, "Send away the ark of the God of Israel, and let it return to its own place, so that it will not kill us and our people." For there was a deadly panic throughout the whole city; the hand of God was very heavy there.
5.12: The men who did not die were struck with tumors, and the cry of the city went up to heaven.

6.1: The ark of the LORD was in Philistine territory for seven months.
6.2: Then the Philistines called for the priests and the diviners and said, "What shall we do with the ark of the LORD? Tell us how we should send it back to its place."
6.3: They said, "If you send away the ark of the God of Israel, do not send it empty-handed, but by all means send a guilt offering with it. Then you will be healed, and you will understand why his hand has not turned away from you."
6.4: They asked, "What guilt offering should we send him?" They answered, "Five golden tumors and five golden mice, corresponding to the number of the Philistine rulers, for the same plague has been on all of you and on your rulers.
6.5: Make images of your tumors and images of the mice that are ravaging your land, and give glory to the God of Israel. Perhaps he will lift his hand from you and your gods and your land.
6.6: Why should you harden your hearts as the Egyptians and Pharaoh hardened their hearts? After he dealt severely with them, did they not let the people go, and they departed?
6.7: Now then, take and prepare a new cart and two milk cows that have never been yoked, and hitch the cows to the cart. Take their calves home, away from them.
6.8: Take the ark of the LORD and place it on the cart, and put the golden figures you are returning as a guilt offering in a box beside it. Then send it off and let it go.
6.9: Then watch. If it goes up toward its own territory, to Beth-shemesh, then it is he who has done us this great harm. But if not, then we will know that it was not his hand that struck us—it happened to us by chance.
6.10: The men did so. They took two milk cows and hitched them to the cart and shut up their calves at home.
6.11: They placed the ark of the LORD on the cart and the chest with the golden mice and the golden images of their tumors.
6.12: And the cows went straight in the direction of Beth-shemesh along one road, lowing as they went. They did not turn to the right or to the left, and the Philistine rulers followed them as far as the border of Beth-shemesh.
6.13: The people of Beth-shemesh were reaping their wheat harvest in the valley. When they looked up and saw the ark, they rejoiced at the sight.
6.14: The cart came to the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh and stopped there. A large stone was there, and they split the wood of the cart and offered the cows as a burnt offering to the LORD.
6.15: The Levites took down the ark of the LORD and the chest beside it containing the golden figures and set them on the large stone. The men of Beth-shemesh offered burnt offerings and made sacrifices to the LORD that day.
6.16: When the five lords of the Philistines saw it, they returned to Ekron that day.
6.17: These are the golden tumors that the Philistines returned as a guilt offering to the LORD: one for Ashdod, one for Gaza, one for Ashkelon, one for Gath, and one for Ekron—
6.18: and the golden mice, according to the number of all the Philistine cities belonging to the five rulers, from fortified cities to unwalled villages. The large stone on which they set down the ark of the LORD is a witness to this day in the field of Joshua of Beth-shemesh.
6.19: But he struck down some of the men of Beth-shemesh because they looked into the ark of the LORD. He struck down seventy of them, and the people mourned because the LORD had struck them with a great blow.
6.20: The men of Beth-shemesh said, "Who is able to stand before the LORD, this holy God? To whom shall he go up from us?"
6.21: So they sent messengers to the people of Kiriath-jearim, saying, "The Philistines have returned the ark of the LORD. Come down and take it up to your place."

19.1: Saul told his son Jonathan and all his servants that they should kill David. But Jonathan, Saul's son, was deeply fond of David.
19.2: Jonathan warned David: 'My father Saul is looking for a way to kill you. Be on guard tomorrow morning—find a hidden place and stay out of sight.'
19.3: 'I will go out and stand near my father in the field where you are, and I will speak to my father about you. Whatever I learn, I will tell you.'
19.4: Jonathan spoke on David's behalf to his father Saul and said to him, 'The king must not wrong his servant David—he has done nothing against you, and his service to you has been outstanding.'
19.5: 'He risked his life and struck down the Philistine, and the LORD won a great victory for all Israel. You saw it yourself and were glad. So why would you sin against innocent blood by killing David for no reason?'
19.6: Saul listened to Jonathan and swore an oath: 'As the LORD lives, David shall not be put to death.'
19.7: Jonathan called David and told him everything; then Jonathan brought David to Saul, and David served before him as before.
19.8: War broke out again, and David went out and fought the Philistines, striking them so hard that they fled before him.
19.9: Then a harmful spirit from the LORD came upon Saul as he sat in his house with his spear in hand, while David was playing the lyre.
19.10: Saul drove the spear at David, trying to pin him to the wall. But David dodged, and the spear stuck in the wall. David fled and got away that night.
19.11: Saul sent agents to David's house to watch him through the night and kill him in the morning. But David's wife Michal warned him: 'If you don't get out tonight, you will be dead by morning.'
19.12: Michal lowered David through a window, and he ran and fled and escaped.
19.13: Michal took a household idol, placed it in the bed, put a cushion of goat hair at the head, and covered it with a garment.
19.14: When Saul's messengers came to take David, she said, 'He is ill.'
19.15: Saul sent the messengers back with orders: 'Bring him to me in his bed—I'll kill him there.'
19.16: When the messengers went in, they found the idol in the bed with a cushion of goat hair where the head should be.
19.17: Saul demanded of Michal, 'Why have you deceived me like this and helped my enemy escape?' Michal replied, 'He threatened me—he said, "Let me go, or I will kill you."'
19.18: David fled and escaped, came to Samuel at Ramah, and told him everything Saul had done to him. And he and Samuel went and stayed at Naioth.
19.19: Word reached Saul: 'David is at Naioth in Ramah.'
19.20: Saul sent agents to seize David. When they arrived and saw the company of prophets prophesying with Samuel standing at their head, the Spirit of God came upon Saul's agents and they too began to prophesy.
19.21: When Saul was told this, he sent another group of agents—and they too prophesied. He sent a third group, and they too prophesied.
19.22: Then Saul himself went to Ramah. He came to the great cistern at Sechu and asked, 'Where are Samuel and David?' Someone said, 'They are at Naioth in Ramah.'
19.23: He set out for Naioth in Ramah—and the Spirit of God came upon him as he walked, and he prophesied all the way until he arrived at Naioth.
19.24: He stripped off his garments and prophesied before Samuel as well, and lay naked all that day and all that night. That is why people say, 'Is Saul also among the prophets?'

20.1: David fled from Naioth in Ramah, came to Jonathan, and asked, 'What have I done? What is my wrongdoing? What sin have I committed against your father, that he is trying to kill me?'
20.2: Jonathan replied, 'Far from it—you will not die! My father never acts, great or small, without telling me. Why would he hide something like this from me? He hasn't.'
20.3: But David insisted with an oath: 'Your father knows that I have found favor with you—and that is precisely why he has decided not to tell you, so you would not be grieved. But I swear by the LORD and by your own life: there is only a step between me and death.'
20.4: Jonathan said to David, 'Whatever you want me to do, I will do it.'
20.5: David said to Jonathan, 'Tomorrow is the new moon festival, and I should be sitting with the king at the feast—but let me go, so I can hide in the field until the third evening.'
20.6: 'If your father notices I'm absent, tell him: "David asked urgently for my permission to go to Bethlehem—there is an annual family sacrifice there."'
20.7: 'If he says "That's fine," then all is well with me. But if he flares up in anger, you'll know he has decided to harm me.'
20.8: 'So show your covenant loyalty to your servant—you yourself brought me into a covenant before the LORD with you. If I have done anything wrong, kill me yourself. Why should you hand me over to your father?'
20.9: Jonathan replied, 'Far from that! If I knew for certain that my father had decided to harm you, I would absolutely tell you.'
20.10: David said to Jonathan, 'Who will let me know if your father answers you harshly?'
20.11: Jonathan said to David, 'Come, let's go out to the field.' And they went out together.
20.12: Jonathan said to David, 'By the LORD, the God of Israel—when I sound out my father tomorrow or the day after, and if the situation looks favorable to David, and I do not immediately send word to let you know,'
20.13: '—then may the LORD punish me, and severely. But if my father means to harm you, I will warn you and send you away safely. May the LORD be with you as he was with my father.'
20.14: 'And if I am still alive, show me the steadfast love of the LORD—don't let me die—'
20.15: '—and never cut off your steadfast love from my house, even when the LORD has cut off all of David's enemies from the face of the earth.'
20.16: So Jonathan made a covenant with the house of David, saying, 'May the LORD hold David's enemies to account.'
20.17: Jonathan made David swear it again—because of his love for David, for he loved him as his own self.
20.18: Jonathan said to him, 'Tomorrow is the new moon feast, and your absence will be noticed—your seat will be empty.'
20.19: 'On the third day go quickly to the place where you hid when this trouble started, and stay beside the stone called Ezel.'
20.20: 'I will shoot three arrows to the side of it, as if I were shooting at a target.'
20.21: 'I will send the boy, saying, "Find the arrows." If I call to him, "Look, the arrows are on this side of you—pick them up," then come out; there is safety for you, no threat. As the LORD lives, it is clear.'
20.22: 'But if I say to the boy, "The arrows are farther out," then go—for the LORD has sent you away.'
20.23: 'And as for everything we have agreed on between us—the LORD is witness between you and me forever.'
20.24: David hid in the field. When the new moon came, the king sat down to eat.
20.25: The king sat in his usual seat against the wall, Jonathan sat opposite, and Abner sat beside Saul—but David's place was vacant.
20.26: Saul said nothing that day, thinking, 'Something has happened to him—he must be ceremonially unclean; yes, he must be unclean.'
20.27: But on the following day, the second day of the month, David's place was still empty. Saul said to his son Jonathan, 'Why hasn't the son of Jesse come to the meal—not yesterday, and not today either?'
20.28: Jonathan replied to Saul, 'David asked my permission urgently to go to Bethlehem.'
20.29: 'He said, "Please let me go—our family has a sacrifice in the city and my brother insisted I be there. If I have found any favor in your eyes, let me slip away to see my brothers." That is why he is not at the king's table.'
20.30: Saul's anger flared at Jonathan, and he shouted at him, 'You son of a twisted, rebellious woman! Do I not know that you have sided with Jesse's son to your own disgrace and to your mother's disgrace?'
20.31: 'For as long as Jesse's son lives on this earth, neither you nor your kingdom will stand. Now send for him and bring him to me—he must die.'
20.32: Jonathan answered his father Saul: 'Why should he die? What has he done?'
20.33: Saul hurled his spear at Jonathan to strike him. That was how Jonathan knew his father was fully determined to kill David.
20.34: Jonathan rose from the table furious and ate nothing that second day of the month, grieving for David because his father had shamed him.
20.35: In the morning Jonathan went out to the field to the appointed place with David, and a young servant went with him.
20.36: He said to the boy, 'Run and find the arrows I shoot.' As the boy ran, Jonathan shot an arrow that flew past him.
20.37: When the boy reached the place where the arrow had landed, Jonathan called out, 'The arrow is farther out, isn't it?'
20.38: Jonathan called to him, 'Hurry! Don't stop!' The boy gathered the arrows and came back to his master.
20.39: The boy knew nothing of the meaning; only Jonathan and David understood the signal.
20.40: Jonathan handed his weapons to the boy and said, 'Go take these back to the city.'
20.41: As soon as the boy was gone, David came out from beside the mound, fell on his face to the ground and bowed three times. Then they kissed each other and wept together, David weeping the most.
20.42: Jonathan said to David, 'Go in peace—we have both sworn in the LORD's name: the LORD shall be between you and me, and between my offspring and yours, forever.' David arose and left, and Jonathan went back into the city.

21.1: David came to Nob, to Ahimelech the priest. Ahimelech came out to meet him trembling and said, 'Why are you alone? Where is everyone?'
21.2: David told the priest, 'The king has sent me on a confidential mission. He instructed me not to tell anyone what he has assigned me to do or where he has sent me. As for my men—I have arranged to meet them at a certain location.'
21.3: 'Now, what do you have available? Give me five loaves of bread, or whatever there is.'
21.4: The priest replied, 'I have no ordinary bread—only the sacred bread. But that I can give you, if your men have kept away from women.'
21.5: David answered, 'Indeed, women have been kept from us as always on campaign. Even on ordinary missions the men keep themselves holy. Certainly today they are clean.'
21.6: So the priest gave him the sacred bread—the only bread available was the bread of the Presence, which is taken from before the LORD and replaced with fresh loaves on the day it is removed.
21.7: A man of Saul's servants happened to be there that day, held before the LORD—Doeg the Edomite, the head of Saul's herdsmen.
21.8: David asked Ahimelech, 'Is there a spear or sword here? I didn't bring my weapons—the king's errand required me to leave quickly.'
21.9: The priest replied, 'The sword of Goliath the Philistine—the one you struck down in the Valley of Elah—is here, wrapped in cloth behind the ephod. If you want it, take it; there's nothing else.' David said, 'There's nothing like it; give it to me.'
21.10: David rose and fled that day from before Saul and went to Achish the king of Gath.
21.11: Achish's servants said to him, 'Isn't this David, the king of the land? Isn't this the one they sing about in their dances: "Saul has struck down his thousands, and David his ten thousands"?'
21.12: David took this seriously and was very afraid of Achish the king of Gath.
21.13: So he altered his behavior before them, acting as if he were insane—scratching at the doors of the gate and letting saliva dribble down his beard.
21.14: Achish said to his servants, 'Look at this man—he's obviously mad! Why did you bring him to me?'
21.15: 'Do I need madmen, that you've brought this one to carry on in front of me? Is this man supposed to come into my palace?'

22.1: David left there and escaped to the cave of Adullam. When his brothers and all his father's household heard, they went down there to him.
22.2: All who were under pressure, all in debt, and all who were bitter came to him, and he became their leader. About four hundred men were with him.
22.3: From there David went to Mizpeh of Moab and asked the king of Moab, 'Please let my father and mother stay with you until I know what God will do for me.'
22.4: He left them in the king of Moab's care, and they stayed there all the while David was in the stronghold.
22.5: The prophet Gad told David, 'Don't stay in the stronghold—go into the land of Judah.' So David left and went to the forest of Hereth.
22.6: Saul heard that David and his men had been located. He was sitting at Gibeah under the tamarisk tree on the hill, his spear in hand, with all his servants standing around him.
22.7: Saul said to his servants, 'Listen, all you Benjaminites! Will Jesse's son give every one of you fields and vineyards? Will he make you commanders over thousands and hundreds?'
22.8: 'Has every one of you conspired against me? No one told me when my own son made a pact with Jesse's son. None of you grieves for me. None of you told me that my son has incited my own servant to ambush me—as he is doing today.'
22.9: Doeg the Edomite, who was standing with Saul's servants, replied, 'I saw Jesse's son come to Nob—to Ahimelech son of Ahitub.'
22.10: 'Ahimelech inquired of the LORD for him, gave him food, and handed over the sword of Goliath the Philistine.'
22.11: The king sent for Ahimelech the priest, Ahitub's son, along with all his father's household—all the priests at Nob. They all came to the king.
22.12: Saul said, 'Hear me, son of Ahitub.' He replied, 'I am here, my lord.'
22.13: 'Why have you conspired against me—you and Jesse's son? You gave him bread and a sword, you inquired of God for him, so that he could rise against me and ambush me, as he is doing now.'
22.14: Ahimelech replied, 'Who among all your servants is as loyal as David? He is your son-in-law, captain of your bodyguard, and one of the most honored people in your household.'
22.15: 'Is this the first time I have inquired of God for him? Of course not! Let the king not charge me or any of my father's house—your servant knew absolutely nothing about any of this, great or small.'
22.16: The king said, 'You will certainly die, Ahimelech—you and your entire father's household.'
22.17: The king ordered his guards who stood beside him, 'Turn and kill the priests of the LORD—they are in league with David; they knew he fled and didn't tell me.' But the king's servants refused to lift their hand against the priests of the LORD.
22.18: The king said to Doeg, 'You—turn and strike the priests.' Doeg the Edomite turned and attacked the priests himself, killing eighty-five men who wore the linen ephod that day.
22.19: And Nob, the city of priests, he put to the sword: men, women, children, infants, oxen, donkeys, and sheep—all with the sword.
22.20: But one son of Ahimelech son of Ahitub escaped—Abiathar—and fled to David.
22.21: Abiathar told David that Saul had killed the priests of the LORD.
22.22: David said to Abiathar, 'I knew that day, when Doeg the Edomite was there, that he would certainly report it to Saul. I am responsible for the death of every person in your father's house.'
22.23: 'Stay with me—don't be afraid. The one who wants my life wants yours too. You are under my protection.'

23.1: David was told, 'The Philistines are attacking Keilah and raiding the threshing floors.'
23.2: David inquired of the LORD: 'Should I go and attack these Philistines?' The LORD replied, 'Go, attack the Philistines, and save Keilah.'
23.3: But David's men objected: 'We're already afraid here in Judah—how much more if we go to Keilah against the Philistines?'
23.4: David inquired of the LORD again. The LORD replied, 'Go down to Keilah—I will give the Philistines into your hand.'
23.5: David and his men went to Keilah, fought the Philistines, drove off their livestock, and struck them hard. David rescued the people of Keilah.
23.6: When Abiathar son of Ahimelech fled to David at Keilah, he brought the ephod with him.
23.7: Saul was told that David had gone to Keilah. He said, 'God has handed him over to me—he has trapped himself by entering a walled city.'
23.8: Saul mobilized all his forces to march on Keilah and besiege David and his men.
23.9: When David learned that Saul was scheming to harm him, he told Abiathar the priest, 'Bring the ephod.'
23.10: David prayed, 'O LORD, God of Israel, your servant has heard that Saul intends to come to Keilah and destroy the city because of me.'
23.11: 'Will the men of Keilah hand me over to him? Will Saul really come, as I have heard? O LORD, God of Israel, tell your servant.' The LORD replied, 'He will come.'
23.12: David asked, 'Will the men of Keilah hand me and my men over to Saul?' The LORD said, 'They will hand you over.'
23.13: David and his men—now about six hundred—arose and left Keilah, moving from place to place as they could. When Saul heard that David had slipped out of Keilah, he called off the campaign.
23.14: David stayed in the wilderness, in the strongholds of the wilderness of Ziph's hill country. Saul hunted him every day, but God did not give David into his hand.
23.15: David learned that Saul had come out to seek his life, while David was at Horesh in the wilderness of Ziph.
23.16: Jonathan, Saul's son, went to David at Horesh and strengthened his resolve in God.
23.17: 'Don't be afraid,' he said. 'My father's hand will not reach you. You will be king over Israel, and I will be second to you—my father knows this himself.'
23.18: The two of them made a covenant before the LORD. David remained at Horesh and Jonathan returned home.
23.19: The Ziphites went up to Saul at Gibeah and said, 'David is hiding among us in the strongholds at Horesh, on the hill of Hachilah south of Jeshimon.'
23.20: 'So come down whenever you like, O king—it's your call. We'll hand him over to you.'
23.21: Saul replied, 'May the LORD bless you for having mercy on me.'
23.22: 'Go and double-check. Find out exactly where he is and who has seen him there—I'm told he is very cunning.'
23.23: 'Find out all his hiding places and come back to me with solid information. Then I will go with you. And if he is anywhere in the land, I will find him—even if I have to search through every clan in Judah.'
23.24: They went ahead of Saul to Ziph. But David and his men were in the wilderness of Maon, in the Arabah south of Jeshimon.
23.25: Saul and his men set out to find David. When David was informed, he went down to the cliff and stayed in the wilderness of Maon. Hearing this, Saul pursued him into that wilderness.
23.26: Saul was going along one side of the mountain while David and his men were on the other side, with David hurrying to escape. Saul and his men were closing the circle around David to capture him.
23.27: But a messenger arrived with urgent news for Saul: 'Come quickly—the Philistines have raided the land.'
23.28: Saul broke off pursuit of David and went to face the Philistines. That is why the place was called the Rock of Escape.
23.29: David went up from there and settled in the strongholds of En-gedi.

24.1: When Saul returned from pursuing the Philistines, he was told, 'David is in the wilderness of En-gedi.'
24.2: Saul took three thousand of Israel's best troops and went to find David and his men near the Rocks of the Wild Goats.
24.3: He came to the sheepfolds along the road where a cave was, and Saul went in to relieve himself. David and his men were sitting in the innermost recesses of the cave.
24.4: David's men whispered to him, 'This is the day the LORD promised—he has given your enemy into your hand. Do whatever seems right to you.' David arose and quietly cut off the corner of Saul's robe.
24.5: Afterward, David's heart struck him because he had cut off the corner of Saul's robe.
24.6: He said to his men, 'The LORD forbid that I should do such a thing to my lord—the LORD's anointed—or put out my hand against him, since he is the LORD's anointed.'
24.7: With these words David held his men back and did not let them attack Saul. Saul got up, left the cave, and went on his way.
24.8: Then David got up, went out of the cave, and called after Saul: 'My lord the king!' Saul looked back, and David bowed to the ground with his face down.
24.9: David said to Saul, 'Why do you give ear to people who say, "David intends to harm you"?'
24.10: 'This very day you have seen with your own eyes that the LORD delivered you into my hands in the cave. Some urged me to kill you, but I spared you. I said, "I will not raise my hand against my lord—he is the LORD's anointed."'
24.11: 'Look, my father—here is the corner of your robe in my hand. I cut it off but I did not kill you. See and understand: there is no evil or treachery in me. I have not wronged you, even though you are hunting me down to take my life.'
24.12: 'May the LORD judge between us—may the LORD avenge me against you—but my hand will never touch you.'
24.13: 'As the old proverb says: "Out of the wicked comes wickedness." But my hand shall not be against you.'
24.14: 'After whom has the king of Israel come out? Who are you chasing? A dead dog! A single flea!'
24.15: 'May the LORD be judge and decide between me and you. May he see my case, plead for me, and deliver me from your power.'
24.16: When David finished speaking, Saul said, 'Is that your voice, my son David?' And Saul wept aloud.
24.17: He said, 'You are more righteous than I am—you have done good to me, but I have done evil to you.'
24.18: 'You have shown today what kind of man you are—when the LORD handed me to you, you let me go.'
24.19: 'If a man finds his enemy, does he let him go safely on his way? May the LORD repay you with good for what you have done to me today.'
24.20: 'Now I know clearly that you will certainly be king, and that the kingdom of Israel will be secured in your hands.'
24.21: 'Swear to me by the LORD that you will not wipe out my family line after me, and that you will not blot out my name from my father's house.'
24.22: David swore this to Saul. Then Saul went home, and David and his men returned to the stronghold.

25.1: Samuel died, and all Israel gathered to mourn him and buried him at his home in Ramah. Then David set out and went down to the wilderness of Paran.
25.2: There was a man in Maon whose property was in Carmel—a very wealthy man with three thousand sheep and a thousand goats. He was at Carmel shearing his sheep.
25.3: His name was Nabal and his wife's name was Abigail. The woman had good understanding and was beautiful, but the man was harsh and wicked in his conduct—a Calebite.
25.4: David heard in the wilderness that Nabal was shearing his sheep.
25.5: David sent ten young men and instructed them, 'Go up to Carmel, find Nabal, and greet him in my name.'
25.6: Say this to him: 'Long life to you! Peace to you, peace to your household, and peace to all that you have!'
25.7: I hear that you are shearing. Your shepherds have been with us, and we have not harassed them or taken anything from them all the time they were at Carmel.
25.8: Ask your servants—they will confirm it. So please let my young men find favor with you, since we come on a feast day. Give whatever you have at hand to your servants and to your son David.
25.9: David's young men arrived and delivered all these words to Nabal in David's name, then waited.
25.10: Nabal answered David's servants, 'Who is David? Who is the son of Jesse? These days there are many servants breaking away from their masters.'
25.11: Why should I take my bread, my water, and the meat I have slaughtered for my shearers and give it to men who come from who knows where?
25.12: David's young men turned and went back, reporting everything Nabal had said.
25.13: David ordered his men, 'Every man strap on your sword!' Every man strapped on his sword, and David did the same. About four hundred men went with David while two hundred remained with the supplies.
25.14: But one of Nabal's servants told Abigail, Nabal's wife, 'David sent messengers from the wilderness to greet our master, and Nabal screamed insults at them.'
25.15: Those men were very good to us; we were not harmed and nothing went missing all the time we were in the field with them.
25.16: They were a wall of protection for us, night and day, all the time we were with them tending the flock.
25.17: So now think about what you are going to do, because harm is coming against our master and his entire household. He is such a difficult man that no one can reason with him.
25.18: Abigail moved quickly. She took two hundred loaves of bread, two skins of wine, five dressed sheep, five seahs of parched grain, a hundred clusters of raisins, and two hundred fig cakes, and loaded them on donkeys.
25.19: She told her servants, 'Go on ahead of me—I will follow you.' She said nothing to her husband Nabal.
25.20: She was riding her donkey coming down the sheltered side of the mountain when she met David and his men coming toward her.
25.21: David had been saying, 'I have been guarding everything this man has in the wilderness for nothing—not a thing went missing—and he has repaid my good with evil!'
25.22: He had sworn, 'May God deal with me, and more, if I leave even one male of Nabal's household alive by morning.'
25.23: When Abigail saw David, she quickly got down from her donkey and fell face down before him, bowing to the ground.
25.24: She fell at his feet and said, 'Let the blame fall on me alone, my lord. Please let your servant speak, and hear me out.'
25.25: Do not let my lord pay attention to this worthless man Nabal. His name means Fool, and folly is exactly what he is. I, your servant, did not see the young men my lord sent.
25.26: And now, my lord, as the LORD lives and as you yourself live—the LORD has kept you from bloodshed and from taking vengeance with your own hand. Now let your enemies and those who seek to harm my lord become like Nabal.
25.27: Let this gift that your servant has brought be distributed to the young men who follow my lord.
25.28: Please forgive your servant's offense. The LORD will certainly establish a lasting house for my lord, because my lord fights the LORD's battles, and no wrong has been found in you throughout your life.
25.29: If anyone rises to pursue you and take your life, the life of my lord will be kept safe and secure in the LORD your God's protection—like something precious bound in a bundle of the living. But the lives of your enemies he will hurl away like a stone from a sling.
25.30: When the LORD has done for my lord all the good he has promised and has appointed you ruler over Israel,
25.31: —you will not have this on your conscience: the guilt of needless bloodshed or of taking vengeance yourself. And when the LORD has dealt well with my lord, please remember your servant.
25.32: David said to Abigail, 'Blessed be the LORD, the God of Israel, who sent you today to meet me!'
25.33: Blessed is your good judgment, and blessed are you yourself—you have kept me from coming to bloodshed today and from taking vengeance with my own hand.
25.34: For truly, as the LORD God of Israel lives, who has held me back from harming you, if you had not come quickly to meet me, not one male of Nabal's household would have been left alive by morning.
25.35: David accepted from her hand what she had brought and said, 'Go home in peace. I have heard your voice and granted your request.'
25.36: Abigail returned to Nabal, and there he was, feasting in his house like a king. He was in high spirits—very drunk—so she said nothing to him until morning.
25.37: In the morning, when Nabal had sobered up, his wife told him everything. His heart failed him and he became like stone.
25.38: About ten days later the LORD struck Nabal, and he died.
25.39: David heard that Nabal was dead and said, 'Blessed be the LORD, who has avenged the insult I received at Nabal's hand and kept his servant from doing evil. The LORD has repaid Nabal's wickedness on his own head.' Then David sent word to Abigail, proposing to take her as his wife.
25.40: David's servants came to Abigail at Carmel and said, 'David has sent us to take you to him as his wife.'
25.41: She arose, bowed to the ground, and said, 'Your servant is a maidservant ready to wash the feet of my lord's servants.'
25.42: Abigail rose quickly, mounted her donkey with five of her serving women, and followed David's messengers. She became his wife.
25.43: David had also taken Ahinoam of Jezreel; both women were his wives.
25.44: Meanwhile Saul had given David's wife Michal to Palti son of Laish, who was from Gallim.

26.1: The Ziphites came to Saul at Gibeah and said, 'Isn't David hiding on the hill of Hachilah, east of the Jeshimon?'
26.2: Saul set out with three thousand specially chosen Israelite soldiers to hunt for David in the wilderness of Ziph.
26.3: Saul pitched camp on the hill of Hachilah along the road east of the Jeshimon. David was in the wilderness and saw that Saul had come after him.
26.4: David sent out scouts and confirmed that Saul had truly arrived.
26.5: David went to the site of Saul's camp and saw where Saul was sleeping—with Abner son of Ner, the army commander, at his side. Saul was lying in the center of the enclosure with the troops camped around him.
26.6: David said to Ahimelech the Hittite and to Abishai son of Zeruiah, Joab's brother, 'Who will go down into the camp with me to Saul?' Abishai said, 'I will.'
26.7: David and Abishai moved through the army at night. Saul was lying asleep in the camp, his spear thrust into the ground at his head, with Abner and the soldiers sleeping around him.
26.8: Abishai whispered to David, 'God has handed your enemy over to you today! Let me pin him to the ground with his own spear—one thrust, that's all it will take.'
26.9: David said, 'No—don't destroy him! Who can lay a hand on the LORD's anointed and not be guilty?'
26.10: David continued, 'As the LORD lives, the LORD himself will strike him down—either his appointed time will come and he'll die naturally, or he'll fall in battle.'
26.11: The LORD forbid that I should stretch out my hand against the LORD's anointed. Now take the spear and the water jug from his head, and let's go.
26.12: David took the spear and the water jug from Saul's head, and they slipped away. No one saw them, no one knew, no one woke up—they were all in a deep sleep, for the LORD had put them into a heavy slumber.
26.13: David crossed to the other side and stood on the top of a hill far away, a great distance between him and the camp.
26.14: David shouted across to the troops and to Abner son of Ner, 'Abner! Are you going to answer?' Abner called back, 'Who are you, calling out to the king?'
26.15: David called back, 'You are a man, aren't you? Who in Israel compares to you? So why haven't you guarded your lord the king? Someone came in to kill the king your lord.'
26.16: 'This is not good what you have done! As the LORD lives, you all deserve death for not guarding your lord, the LORD's anointed! Now look—where is the king's spear? Where is the water jug that was by his head?'
26.17: Saul recognized David's voice and called out, 'Is that your voice, David my son?' David answered, 'It is my voice, my lord the king.'
26.18: David said, 'Why is my lord pursuing his servant? What have I done? What harm is in my hands?'
26.19: Please let my lord the king hear his servant out. If the LORD has moved you against me, may he accept an offering. But if men have done this, may they be cursed before the LORD! They have driven me away today so I have no portion in the LORD's inheritance—as if they were telling me to go worship other gods.
26.20: Do not let my blood fall on the ground far from the LORD's presence. The king of Israel has come out to look for a single flea—like someone hunting a partridge in the hills.
26.21: Saul said, 'I have sinned. Come back, David my son—I will not harm you again because you held my life precious today. I have played the fool and made a terrible mistake.'
26.22: David called back, 'Here is the king's spear! Send one of your men to come get it.'
26.23: The LORD rewards every man for his righteousness and loyalty. The LORD handed you over to me today, and I would not lay a hand on the LORD's anointed.
26.24: Just as I valued your life today, may the LORD value my life and rescue me from all distress.
26.25: Saul said to David, 'Blessed be you, my son David! You will accomplish great things and will surely succeed.' David went on his way, and Saul returned home.

27.1: David thought to himself, 'I am going to die by Saul's hand one of these days. My best option is to escape into Philistia. Then Saul will stop searching for me throughout Israel, and I will be out of his reach.'
27.2: So David set out with his six hundred men and crossed over to Achish son of Maoch, king of Gath.
27.3: David settled at Gath with Achish, he and his men, each with his household. David brought his two wives—Ahinoam of Jezreel and Abigail, the widow of Nabal of Carmel.
27.4: When Saul heard that David had fled to Gath, he stopped searching for him.
27.5: David said to Achish, 'If you are pleased with me, assign me a place in one of the country towns to live there. Why should your servant live in the royal city with you?'
27.6: Achish gave him Ziklag that day. That is why Ziklag has belonged to the kings of Judah to this day.
27.7: David lived in Philistine territory for a year and four months.
27.8: David and his men would raid the Geshurites, the Girzites, and the Amalekites—peoples who had long inhabited the region from the direction of Shur toward Egypt.
27.9: Whenever David attacked a region he left no one alive—man or woman—taking the sheep, oxen, donkeys, camels, and clothing before returning to Achish.
27.10: Achish would ask, 'Where did you raid today?' And David would say, 'Against the Negeb of Judah,' or 'Against the Negeb of the Jerahmeelites,' or 'Against the Negeb of the Kenites.'
27.11: David never brought back any prisoners to Gath; he thought, 'If survivors reached Gath, they would tell what David really did.' This was his regular practice throughout his time in Philistine territory.
27.12: Achish completely trusted David, thinking, 'He has made himself utterly repulsive to his own people Israel; he will be my servant permanently.'

28.1: In those days the Philistines gathered their forces to fight Israel. Achish told David, 'Understand clearly—you and your men are going out with me into battle.'
28.2: David said, 'Then you will see what your servant can do.' Achish replied, 'Good—I will appoint you as my permanent bodyguard.'
28.3: Samuel was dead—all Israel had mourned him and buried him in Ramah, his own city. Saul had previously expelled mediums and necromancers from the land.
28.4: The Philistines assembled and camped at Shunem; Saul gathered all Israel and camped at Gilboa.
28.5: Saul looked at the Philistine forces and was terrified—his heart shaking with dread.
28.6: Saul inquired of the LORD, but the LORD did not answer him—not through dreams, not through the Urim, not through prophets.
28.7: Saul told his servants, 'Find me a woman who is a medium, so I can go consult her.' His servants replied, 'There is a medium at Endor.'
28.8: Saul disguised himself, changed his clothes, and went with two men to the woman at night. He said, 'Consult a spirit for me, and bring up whoever I tell you.'
28.9: The woman said, 'Surely you know what Saul has done—he has eliminated all the mediums and necromancers from the land. Why are you setting a trap for my life to get me killed?'
28.10: Saul swore to her by the LORD, 'As the LORD lives, no punishment will come to you for this.'
28.11: The woman asked, 'Who shall I bring up for you?' He answered, 'Bring up Samuel.'
28.12: When the woman saw Samuel, she cried out with a loud voice and said to Saul, 'Why did you deceive me? You are Saul!'
28.13: The king said, 'Do not be afraid. What do you see?' The woman said, 'I see a divine figure rising up from the earth.'
28.14: He asked, 'What does he look like?' She said, 'An old man, wrapped in a robe.' Saul knew it was Samuel and bowed with his face to the ground.
28.15: Samuel said to Saul, 'Why have you disturbed me by bringing me up?' Saul answered, 'I am in desperate straits. The Philistines are fighting me, and God has turned away and no longer answers me—not by prophets, not by dreams. So I called you to tell me what to do.'
28.16: Samuel replied, 'Why ask me? The LORD has turned away from you and become your adversary.'
28.17: The LORD has done to you exactly what he said through me—he has torn the kingdom out of your hand and given it to your neighbor David.
28.18: Because you did not obey the LORD and did not carry out his fierce judgment against Amalek—that is why the LORD has done this to you today.
28.19: The LORD will hand Israel along with you over to the Philistines. Tomorrow you and your sons will be with me. The LORD will give Israel's army into the Philistines' hand.
28.20: Saul immediately fell full length on the ground, overwhelmed with fear at Samuel's words. There was no strength left in him—he had eaten nothing all day or all night.
28.21: The woman came to Saul, and seeing how shaken he was, she said, 'Look, your servant has done what you asked. I put my life at risk to obey you.'
28.22: Now please listen to me—let me set some food before you so you have the strength to go on your way.
28.23: He refused, saying 'I will not eat.' But his servants and the woman pressed him until he gave in. He got up from the ground and sat on the bed.
28.24: The woman had a fattened calf in the house; she quickly slaughtered it, took flour, kneaded it, and baked unleavened bread.
28.25: She set the food before Saul and his servants, and they ate. Then they rose and left that same night.

29.1: The Philistines assembled all their forces at Aphek while Israel was encamped by the spring at Jezreel.
29.2: The Philistine lords were passing in review by hundreds and thousands. David and his men were marching at the rear with Achish.
29.3: The Philistine commanders objected, 'What are these Hebrews doing here?' Achish defended him: 'Is this not David, who served Saul king of Israel? He has been with me for years now—I have found nothing against him since the day he came over to me.'
29.4: But the commanders were angry and said, 'Send him back to the place you gave him—he must not march with us into battle. He could turn against us in the fight. How better to win back his lord's favor than with our heads?'
29.5: Is this not the David they sing about in their dances: 'Saul has killed his thousands, and David his ten thousands'?
29.6: Achish called David and said, 'As the LORD lives, you have been trustworthy—I would be glad to have you in the campaign, for I have found nothing wrong with you since the day you came to me. But the lords will not allow it.'
29.7: So go back now in peace, so as not to offend the Philistine lords.
29.8: David protested to Achish, 'But what have I done? What have you found against me from the day I came to you until now, that I shouldn't fight against the enemies of my lord the king?'
